azberjibiou's blog

Codeforces Round #851 (Div. 2) Editorial
By azberjibiou, 3 years ago,

1788A - One and Two

Problem idea: azberjibiou

Tutorial

1788A - Единицы и двойки

There should be same number of 22 at a1,a2,⋯aka1,a2,⋯ak and ak+1,⋯,anak+1,⋯,an.

By checking every kk, we can solve the problem at O(N2)O(N2). By sweeping kk from 11 to n−1n−1, we can solve the problem in O(N)O(N). Not counting the number of 22 but naively multiplying using sweeping in python was accepted since it has time complexity O(N2)O(N2).

Checking every kk and naively multiplying solutions are O(N3)O(N3), so those solutions won't fit in time limit.

1788B - Sum of Two Numbers

Problem idea: azberjibiou

Tutorial

1788B - Сумма двух чисел

Let's assume that there is no carry while adding xx and yy. Denote n=a9⋯a1a0n=a9⋯a1a0, x=b9⋯b1b0x=b9⋯b1b0, y=c9⋯c1c0y=c9⋯c1c0 in decimal system. The condition can be changed as the following condition.

- ai=bi+ciai=bi+ci for all 0≤i≤90≤i≤9. - Sum of bibi and sum of cici should differ by at most 11.

If aiai is even, let bi=ci=ai/2bi=ci=ai/2. Otherwise, let bibi and cici be ai+12ai+12 or ai−12ai−12. By alternating between (bi,ci)=(ai+12,ai−12)(bi,ci)=(ai+12,ai−12) and (bi,ci)=(ai−12,ai+12)(bi,ci)=(ai−12,ai+12), we can satisfy the condition where sum of bibi and sum of cici differ by at most 11.

There is an alternative solution. If nn is even, divide it into (n2,n2n2,n2). If remainder of nn divided by 1010 is not 99, divide it into (n+12,n−12n+12,n−12). If remainder of nn divided by 1010 is 99, recursively find an answer for ⌊n10⌋⌊n10⌋ which is (x′,y′x′,y′) and the answer will be (10x′+4,10y′+510x′+4,10y′+5) or (10x′+5,10y′+410x′+5,10y′+4) depending on what number has a bigger sum of digits.

The following solution has a countertest.

1. Trying to find xx and yy by bruteforce from (1,n−11,n−1). 2. Trying to find xx and yy by bruteforce from (n+12,n−12n+12,n−12)

A solution that randomly finds (x,yx,y) passes.

1788C - Matching Numbers

Problem idea: azberjibiou

Tutorial

1788C - Разбиение на пары

Let's assume that 11 to 2n2n is paired and each sum of pair is k,k+1,⋯,k+n−1k,k+1,⋯,k+n−1. Sum from 11 to 2n2n should equal to the sum of kk to k+n−1k+n−1. So we obtain n(2n+1)=(2k+n−1)n2n(2n+1)=(2k+n−1)n2, which leads to 4n+2=2k+n−14n+2=2k+n−1. Since 4n+24n+2 is even, 2k+n−12k+n−1 should be even. So if nn is even, we cannot find such pairing.

If nn is odd, there are various ways to make such pairing. Let m=n−12m=n−12. (1,3m+3),(2,3m+4),…(m,4m+2),(m+1,2m+2),(m+2,2m+3),…(2m+1,3m+2)(1,3m+3),(2,3m+4),…(m,4m+2),(m+1,2m+2),(m+2,2m+3),…(2m+1,3m+2) can be such pairing.

1788D - Moving Dots

Problem idea: azberjibiou

Tutorial

1788D - Moving Dots

Let's think about the original problem where we do not think about subsets.

We can easily observe that each dot does not change direction during moving.

Assume that dots gather at coordinate xx. Rightmost dot of dots that have smaller coordinate than xx should move right, and leftmost dot which has bigger coordinate than xx should move left. We can observe that the number of adjacent dot where each move toward each other will be the answer.

Now let's solve the problem for subsets. Instead of counting number of adjacent dot that moves toward each other for each subset of dots, we will count the number of subset for each possible 1≤i<j≤N1≤i<j≤N where dot ii moves right and dot jj moves left and there are no dots between ii and jj.

Let the coordinate of a dot in a subset be kk. We will try to find out which kk can be in a subset where dot ii and dot jj move toward each other.

Since there are no dot between ii and jj, dots satisfying xi<k<xjxi<k<xj should not be in the subset. Since dot ii should move right, dots that satisfy k<xik<xi and xi−k≤xj−xixi−k≤xj−xi should not be in the subset. As the same way for dot jj, dots that satisfy k>xjk>xj and k−xj<xj−xik−xj<xj−xi should not be in the subset. Summing these up, dots satisfying 2xi−−xj≤k<2xj−−xi2xi−−xj≤k<2xj−−xi should not be in the subset. By using binary search, we can find the number of dots that cannot be in the subset in O(logN)O(logN).

If there are pp dots that can be in the subset, the number of subset where ii and jj moves toward each other will be 2p2p. Summing all 2p2p will give us the answer. Since there are O(N2)O(N2) pairs of (i,j)(i,j), we can solve the problem in O(N2logN)O(N2logN).

Instead of using binary search, we can use the monotonicity of 2xj−xi2xj−xi and 2xi−xj2xi−xj when jj increases, we can solve the range of dots that cannot be in the subset in O(N2)O(N2) by sweeping. Both O(N2logN)O(N2logN) and O(N2)O(N2) solutions will be accepted.

There were some dynamic programming solutions from some testers.

1788E - Sum Over Zero

Problem idea: azberjibiou

Problem solver: YeongTree

Tutorial

1788E - Сумма больше нуля

Denote pp as the prefix sum of aa. For a segment [x+1,y][x+1,y] to be an element of SS, px≤pypx≤py should be satisfied.

Let's denote dpidpi as the maximum value of the sum of length of segment smaller than ii in SS. Segment [x,y][x,y] is smaller than ii if y≤iy≤i. If there is no segment ending at ii, dpi=dpi−1dpi=dpi−1. If there is segment [k+1,i][k+1,i] in SS, dpi=maxpk≤pi(dpk+i−k)dpi=maxpk≤pi(dpk+i−k). By summing up, dpi=max(dpi−1,maxpk≤pi(dpk+i−k)dpi=max(dpi−1,maxpk≤pi(dpk+i−k) With this dp, we get an O(N2)O(N2) solution.

Now let's try to speed up the dp transition using segment tree. First, use coordinate compression on pipi since we only see whether one prefix sum is bigger than the other. We will maintain a segment tree that stores dpk−kdpk−k in position pkpk.

Let's find dpidpi in order of ii. dpi=max(dpi−1,maxpk≤pi(dpk−k)+i)dpi=max(dpi−1,maxpk≤pi(dpk−k)+i) We can solve maxpk≤pi(dpk−k)maxpk≤pi(dpk−k) by range query [0,pi][0,pi] on a segment tree. So we can solve dpidpi in O(logN)O(logN) for each ii.

The entire problem is solved in O(NlogN)O(NlogN).

There is an alternative solution that maintains pair (dpk−k,pk)(dpk−k,pk) monotonically with a set. This solution also runs in O(NlogN)O(NlogN).

1788F - XOR, Tree, and Queries

Problem idea: azberjibiou

Tutorial

1788F - XOR, деревья и запросы

Let's denote pipi as xor of edges in path from node 11 to node ii. Edges in path from ii to jj is

(edges in path from 11 to ii) + (edges in path from 11 to jj) — 2×2×(edges in path from 11 to lca(i,j)lca(i,j))

where lca(i,j)lca(i,j) denotes the least common ancestor of ii and jj. Since xor of two same number is 00, we can observe that xor of path from ii to jj is pi⊕pjpi⊕pj.

If we know every pipi for all ii, weight of edge connecting ii and jj is pi⊕pjpi⊕pj, so deciding weight of every edge is equivalent to deciding every pipi for i≥2i≥2.

Let GG be the original tree graph. Let's make a undirected weighted graph G′G′ from the given condition. Condition (uu, vv, xx) makes an edge connecting uu and vv with weight xx. This edge means that pv=pu⊕xpv=pu⊕x. Now if uu and vv are connected by edges in G′G′, this means that pv=pu⊕pv=pu⊕ (xor of edges in path from uu to vv).

First, let's check if it is possible to make a graph that satisfies the conditions.

If G′G′ is a connected graph, we can solve pipi using dfs from node 11. For edges not used in dfs (back edges), we have to check if it is valid with the solved pipi. Specifically, if there is an edge (uu, vv, xx), we need to check if pu⊕pv=xpu⊕pv=x.

Now let's think when G′G′ is not a connected graph. By dividing the graph into connected components, we can solve if it is impossible to make a graph that satisfies the conditions in a same fashion.

Now let's try to minimize a1⊕a2⊕⋯⊕an−1a1⊕a2⊕⋯⊕an−1.

For edge (u,v)(u,v) in GG, weight of edge is pu⊕pvpu⊕pv. By substituting aiai into pxi⊕pxi⊕ pyipyi, a1⊕a2⊕⋯⊕an−1a1⊕a2⊕⋯⊕an−1 can be written as xor of pipi where node ii has odd degree. Let the connected components of G′G′ be G1,G2,…,GkG1,G2,…,Gk. Pick any vertex rkrk in each component GkGk. For every vertex ii in GkGk, pipi is expressed as prk⊕prk⊕ (xor of edges in path from rkrk to ii).

Let's define a set LL as L=L={i|Gii|Gi has odd number of odd degree vertices}.

By substituting pipi into (prk⊕prk⊕ (xor of edges in path in G′G′ from rkrk to ii)), we can rewrite "xor of pipi where node ii has odd degree" into ⨁k∈Lprk⊕c⨁k∈Lprk⊕c where cc is a constant.

If LL is empty, the answer is constant and any solution that satisfy the given conditions will be the answer.

If LL is not empty, set one of prkprk to cc and other prkprk as 00 so that a1⊕a2⊕⋯an−1a1⊕a2⊕⋯an−1 is 00.

We can solve the problem in O(N)O(N).

Fast O(NlogX)O(NlogX) solution might be accepted, including the solution where you divide the weight of edge by bits.

Tutorial of Codeforces Round 851 (Div. 2)

← Rev. 2 →  
+8

Tutorial for Problem B:

Hint 1

Think in term of digits

Hint 2

Can we divide each digit by 2 and then assign in the two numbers.

Solution

Let a and b be empty arrays , from which we form number a1 and b1 later.

Traverse from the least significant digit ,Divide each digit by 2 ,now two cases arise :

Case1: Digit is even, Then simply push digit/2 in a and b.

Case2: Digit is odd ,As We have to maintain the sum of digits of a and b equal to 0 or 1 ,so if there are multiple odd digits then we have to maintain a flag such that we can push (digit+1)/2 and (digit)/2 alternatively in number a and b.

for eg .n= 134

we traverse the number in the way 431 :

initially a={ } b={ }

FOR digit 4 , a={2} b={2} 4/2=2 (case of even digit)

For digit 3 , a={2,2} b={2,1} as (3+1)/2=2 and (3/2)=1 (case of odd digit).

for digit 1 a={2,2,0} b={2,1,1} as we have already push for an odd number in a then this time we will push (digit+1)/2 in b.

So final number formed from a is a1=220 and from b is b1= 211 (i am assuming you may know how to form a number from given digits.)

mycode . Note : this is one of the approach ,there can be multiple optimized approaches.

apologize for my shit English.

→ Reply

+97

C, D and F are great problems. I think the editorial for C kinda glosses over inspiration for the construction.

Here is my construction idea

Consider

14 13 12 11 10 9  8
1  2  3  4  5  6  7

Currently all pairs have equal sums. If I swap the bottom elements of 2 pairs with difference dd, then the sum of one pair increases by dd, and the other reduces by dd. Over here if I am able to get +d+d and −d−d for dd from 00 to 33, it will produce consecutive sums.

14 13 12 11 10 9  8
1  2  3  4  5  6  7
|  |  |  |  |     |
|  |__|  |  |     |
|________|  |_____|

Notice that the odd differences are nested within each other, and so are the even differences. Swapping the shown pairs produces.

14 13 12 11 10 9  8
4  3  2  1  7  6  5

This has consecutive sums from 1212 to 1818. Generalising to arbitrary odd nn is not difficult.

This also makes it trivial to see, that even nn is impossible. You can produce any pairing by swapping elements. Each swap will make one pair increase by xx, and the other decrease by xx. Let us consider the final solution. Each final pair will be 2n+1+x2n+1+x for xx in some consecutive range, that adds to 00. This is impossible for an even length range. If there are more positive elements, the range will be positive, and negative otherwise. This is very trivial to check. Therefore even nn is impossible.

    Nice idea, maybe better than my construction idea.

    Here is my construction idea.

    For some integer xx and yy, (x,y),(x+1,y+1),…,(x+k,y+k)(x,y),(x+1,y+1),…,(x+k,y+k) will give an arithmetic sequence of common difference 22.

    Arithmetic sequence from x1+y1x1+y1 will cover the even part, and another arithmetic sequence from x2+y2x2+y2 will cover the odd part. After some work, you can find x1,y1,x2,y2x1,y1,x2,y2.

    → Reply

    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 4 →  
    0

    Hi Everule,

    I think I have a much simpler construction for this problem. A bit of math will tell you that the smallest sum will be 3(n + 1)/2.

    Let,
    S = 3(n + 1)/2

    Now break up the sequence [1, 2n] in two parts [1, n] and [n + 1, 2n].
    First pair is (1, S — 1)
    Second pair is (2, S — 1 + 1)
    and so on...
    Just make sure to loop around the first number of the pair in the range [1, n] and second number in the range [n + 1, 2n]. You will generate all sums in the range [S, S + n — 1].

    Proof left as an exercise for the reader. :)

    https://codeforces.com/contest/1788/submission/193235034

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      yh11
      3 years ago, show #
      »
      »
      »
      yh11
      3 years ago, hide # ^ |
       
      +11

      Adding proof because some people asked for it.

      Lemma 1: 

      Smallest sum (S) = 3(n + 1)/2

      Lemma 2:

      S - 1 is the middle number, i.e, the ceil(n / 2)th number in the range [n + 1, 2n], so if [n + 1, 2n] had 5 numbers S - 1 would be the 3rd. For example if [n + 1, 2n] was [6, 10], S - 1 would be 8.

      Terminology:

      Lets call sums with even difference from S, even sums.
      Lets call sums with odd differences from S, odd sums.
      So [S, S + 2, S + 4, S + 6, ...] are even sums.
      And [S + 1, S + 3, S + 5, ...] are odd sums.

      Main Proof:
      We have to make n sums in the range [S, S + n - 1], since according to Lemma 1, smallest sum must be S and we have to make n consecutive sums.

      So, we have to make ceil(n / 2) even sums and floor(n / 2) odd sums.
      Visually, we have to make 

       S   S + 1  S + 2  S + 3  ... S + n - 1  
 ^     ^      ^      ^           ^
even  odd    even   odd         even 
      S + n - 1 will have same parity as S, since n is odd. (This also comes from Lemma 1.)

      Lets take this as our first pair.
      [1, S - 1], clearly this makes sum S one of even sum.

      Next pairs will be -

      [2, S],
      [3, S + 1] ....
      [ceil(n / 2), 2n]

      This is the key point.
      The last pair will have [ceil(n / 2), 2n] because of Lemma #2. S - 1 was the middle number and we move till end so the first number in the pair becomes ceil(n / 2).
      Consequently, we have ceil(n / 2) pairs and the sum of each pair is greater than the previous by 2, so we have generated all the Even Sums.

      After this we are going to loop around the range [n + 1, 2n] for the second number of the pair.

      So, next pair will be
      [ceil(n / 2) + 1, n + 1]

      The sum will be (n + 1) / 2 + 1 + n + 1 = S + 1.

      Now if we keep generating subsequent pairs until we reach
      [n, S - 2], we will have generated all the odd sums starting from S + 1.

      Both Lemmas can be easily proved with a bit of math.

      → Reply
  • »

+3

For B, I brute forced many numbers and eventually found a pattern

For even you can just do n / 2, n / 2 For odd you will find answer (n + 1)/2 + x, (n — 1)/2 — x Where x = 4, 49, 499, 4999, ..., 8, 89, 899, 8999, ...

Can somebody please prove my solution?

#include <bits/stdc++.h>
#define int long long int

void solve(){
    int n;
    std::cin >> n;

    auto f = [&] (int x) {
        int s = 0;
        while(x) {
            s += x % 10;
            x /= 10;
        }
        return s;
    };

    if(std::abs(f(n / 2) - f(n - n / 2)) <= 1) {
        std::cout << n / 2 << " " << n - n / 2 << "\n";
        return;
    }

    int i = 4, x = (n + 1) / 2, y = (n - 1) / 2;
    while(i < n) {
        if(std::abs(f(x + i) - f(y - i)) <= 1) {
            std::cout << x + i << " " << y - i << "\n";
            return;
        }
        i = i * 10 + 9;
    }
    i = 8;
    while(i < n) {
        if(std::abs(f(x + i) - f(y - i)) <= 1) {
            std::cout << x + i << " " << y - i << "\n";
            return;
        }
        i = i * 10 + 9;
    }
}
     
signed main() {

    std::ios::sync_with_stdio(false);
    std::cin.tie(0);

    #ifndef ONLINE_JUDGE
    freopen("input.txt", "r", stdin);
    freopen("output.txt", "w", stdout);
    #endif // ONLINE_JUDGE

    int t = 1;
    std::cin >> t;
    for(int i = 1; i <= t; i++) {
        solve();
    }
}
→ Reply

  • »
    »
    info
    3 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    info
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 3 →  
    +19

    Here's how alternate solution you said works.

    Note the recurrence in the official solution:

    dpi=max(dpi−1,maxpk≤pi(dpk−k)+i)dpi=max(dpi−1,maxpk≤pi(dpk−k)+i)

    Solving it with brute-force costs $O(n^2)$ time. But we can solving it in almost linear time with a technique similar to Monotonous Queue.

    We find that when there's a pair of index (x,y)(x,y) s.t. dpx−x<dpy−ydpx−x<dpy−y while px≥pypx≥py, if we use k=xk=x in one recurrence, we can always use k=yk=y (since py≤px≤pipy≤px≤pi) with an better value, so xx can never be the optimal decision in a recurrence.

    We use a set to maintain all pair (dpk−k,pk)(dpk−k,pk) (sorting by pkpk) possibly optimal for a recurrence. When adding ii, we can maintain it by erasing all xx with dpx−x<dpi−i,px≥pidpx−x<dpi−i,px≥pi, and check if ii will be erased after adding ii. And then it satisfies something similar to Monotonous Queue: if px<pypx<py then dpx−x<dpy−ydpx−x<dpy−y. So we can search for the maximum pkpk for the optimal decision(O(logn)O(log⁡n) per recurrence).

    code (Note that this solution calculate the minimum count of numbers not present in any segment you choose instead.)

    → Reply

In E, what does it mean to "use coordinate compression on pipi" where pp is the prefix sum array of aa ?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    tyagsa
    3 years ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    tyagsa
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    +3

    precompute all the possible prefixes of the given array and then map them to the range 0 — k-1 based on their relative values (k is the number of unique prefixes). eg- if the prefixes or -3 4 2 -1 then this will be converted to 0 3 2 1.

    My Submission

    my solution was something like dp[i][j][dir] -> number of collisions if you are standing on the ith index, you chose the jth index before you and the jth index had a direction left/right.

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      Pepe_Roni
      3 years ago, show (+1) #
      »
      »
      »
      Pepe_Roni
      3 years ago, hide # ^ |

      what would the transitions be?

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        tyagsa
        3 years ago, show #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        tyagsa
        3 years ago, hide # ^ |
        ← Rev. 2 →  
        0

        let K be the first index such that dist[i]-dist[j] <= dist[k]-dist[i]

        dp[i][j][dir] = dp[i+1][i][right] + ... dp[k-1][i][right] + dp[k][i][left] + ... dp[n-1][i][left] + {2^(n-k) if (dir == right) because for all these subsets we will have a collision point between i and j }

        My Submission

        → Reply

      Thank you. Can you clarify a bit more about what do you mean by, you chose the j-th index before you and the j-th index had a direction left/right. But the problem says each dot moves in the direction of the closest dot (different from itself) until it meets another dot. So doesn't it mean each dot has their directions fixed ?

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        tyagsa
        3 years ago, show #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        tyagsa
        3 years ago, hide # ^ |
         
        0

        I am assuming that

        1. There is no element chosen between the j'th and the i'th element
        2. The j'th element has its direction fixed ie. either its moving right towards the ith element (which is closest for it), or left (to some other closer element on its left as compared to the ith element, we dont care what that element is, only the direction matters to us).

        My final answer is the sum of dp[i][[j][right] for all pairs of i and j.

        If you are thinking that maybe the dp[i][j][left] is not a valid state because j has no closer element on its left w.r.t to i. Then that state will never be calculated in our answer.

For problem F, in the formula ⨁k∈Lprk⊕c⨁k∈Lprk⊕c, the value of cc is the xor-sum of every path from prkprk to every vertex in GkGk, right?

Then, wouldn't changing the value of prkprk also change the values of pipi for some vertices in GkGk? If I understand this correctly, we are getting the initial values of pipi with dfs in each component of G′G′, setting its value to the xor-path from the dfs root to vertex ii. So, if rkrk is in the middle of the path, the value of pipi would also change.

I'm guessing that I'm not understanding that part correctly, where does the constant cc comes from? Is that the way to get the initial values of pipi?

Thanks.

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    azberjibiou
    3 years ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    azberjibiou
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +3

    If you change prkprk, pipi would also change. Here is one example.

    If G′G′ has 33 vertices, rk=2rk=2 and the edges are (1,2,3),(2,3,1)(1,2,3),(2,3,1)

    p3=p2⊕1p3=p2⊕1 and p1=p2⊕3p1=p2⊕3.

    Odd degree vertices are 11 and 33, so we get p1⊕p3=p2⊕1⊕p2⊕3=2p1⊕p3=p2⊕1⊕p2⊕3=2

    In this case, c=2c=2.

With how vague the editorial is about finding a matching for C, I'm convinced that it's really just a pattern-finding problem after the initial steps of removing even nn from consideration and finding the target sums for odd nn. Now I don't feel so bad for not being able to solve it.

For those who did solve C, can anyone please elaborate on the thought process on how you arrived at a correct matching?

    Pattern finding is the case for me. I first tried getting the sum of (a1+b1)(a1+b1) with a little bit of math which turned out to be (3n+3)2(3n+3)2 and noticed that from pair (ai,bi)(ai,bi), we can get to the next sum (ai+1,bi+1)(ai+1,bi+1) with a difference of 11 by adding +2+2 to aiai and −1−1 to bibi. After randomly testing many ways to do the matching, setting a1=1a1=1 and b1=(3n+3)2−1b1=(3n+3)2−1 worked. I also stress-tested this with a bunch of random test cases to confirm. (I left out some details which you can check in my submission if interested).

can anyone please tell what i am doing wrong, wrong answer on test 2 , 61st numbers differ — expected: '316', found: '71' 193009289

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    Andalus
    3 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    Andalus
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +5

    You're trying to compute the products, but this would not even fit long long if there are many 2s. If there are 1000 22s, the result is 2100021000, which requires around 1000 bits. The long long type only supports 64 bits.

    Rather than calculate the products directly, I would suggest that you instead keep track of the power of 2 that the product has, i.e., how many 2s were present in this desired product.

    → Reply
»

Can someone explain the solution of D problem? Thanks in advance

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    Andalus
    3 years ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    Andalus
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +13

    Suppose you take a specific subset of ≥2≥2 dots. You can then represent each dot as L or R based on whether it moves left or right, and construct a string of Ls and Rs. String always begins with R and ends with L. The number of distinct stopping coordinates for this subset is equal to the number of instances of the substring "RL" (each R stops at the same place as its next L, and each L stops at the same place as its previous R).

    Therefore, the objective is to output the sum of the number of RL substrings in ALL possible subsets of size 2. However, this can be rephrased as follows: for each pair of dots ii and jj, count the total number of subsets where dot ii and dot jj form an RL substring, and output the sum for all pairs.

    Given ii and jj, when will dot ii and dot jj form an RL substring? This arises only in a subset for which dot jj is the closest dot to dot ii (no ties allowed, since ii breaks ties by going left, away from jj) and dot ii is the closest dot to dot jj (ties allowed, since jj breaks ties by going left, towards ii). To count how many of these there, we can first count the number of dots, excluding ii and jj, such that the presence of these dots will not prevent ii and jj from moving towards each other.

    Let d=xj−xid=xj−xi. Dots with positions <xi−d<xi−d are allowed, since ii still moves to jj, and the dots with positions ≥xj+d≥xj+d are also allowed, since jj still moves to ii. We can count how many such dots there are with two binary searches: one for xi−dxi−d and another for xj+dxj+d. If we have ss such dots, then there are 2s2s subsets of these ss dots that we can include alongside ii and jj to generate a subset (of all dots) where ii and jj form an RL pair.

    The final output is the sum of 2s2s for each (i,j)(i,j) pair, modulo 109+7109+7.

    My submission: 193040606. The pw vector stores the powers of 2 modulo 109+7109+7. The variables lsz and rsz denote the number of allowed dots that are left of ii and right of jj respectively. The way the built-in lower_bound is defined conveniently fits with how ties are handled in this problem.

    → Reply

0

I did not find the similar approach for C so here it is:
Let's pair all numbers at 1..n(left) with numbers from n+1..2n(right).
Let's start with 1 and some x, their sum is (x + 1) so for next pair sum should be (x + 2). I can't take 2 as next left number cause then I must take x as right number for sum to be (x + 2). So I take 3 as next left number and x-1 as right number. Next pair will be 5 and x-2 and so on. That's how I construct pair with odd numbers from 1..n: I increase left by 2 and decrease right by 1 to make step=1 in sums of pairs.
Then I need to deal with even numbers. After last odd number I need to go to first even number 2. It means I need to decrease left by (last_odd — 2) and increase right by (last_odd — 2) + 1.
Then again I increase left by 2 to iterate over even numbers and decrease right by 1 to make step=1 in sums of pairs.
To make it all work we need to choose x at the beginning. The most critical part is when we go from last odd number to first even number: we need to increase right by (last_odd — 2) + 1 which is equals to n-1 because last_odd=n. So to not overflow 2n limit we need to have right number as 2n-(n-1)=n+1 at most. Apparently this is the least right number we can get from n+1..2n.
So x at the beginning should be (n+1)+(count_odd_numbers_in_1..n — 1)

examples:

n=3

1 5
3 4
2 6

n=5

1 8
3 7
5 6
2 10
4 9

n=7

1 11
3 10
5 9
7 8
2 14
4 13
6 12

submission 192961794

→ Reply

I have a question about Problem B: Since I need to find 2 numbers, A and B, for the input number N such that, a) A + B = N and b) sumofdigits(A) — sumofdigits(B) is at most 1, could I not just use the approach where I do the following:

  • if N is even, assign N/2 to both A and B; --> ensures that sumofdigits(A) — sumofdigits(B) = 0
  • if N is odd, assign N/2 to A, and (N/2)+1 to B; --> ensures that sumofdigits(A) — sumofdigits(B) = 1

Could someone please clarify this for me, since I think I misunderstood the question?

  • »

    Andalus
    3 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    Andalus
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    I think you understood the question, but your approach is incorrect. Consider N=19N=19, where N/2=9N/2=9 (whose digits sum to 9) and N/2+1=10N/2+1=10 (whose digits sum to 1). There are many other edge cases (like if you have consecutive 9s) that this kind of approach would not be suitable.

    long cannot store values larger than 263−1263−1. Due to how integer overflow works, after the true product you're trying to store in currProd becomes 264264, the actual value stored in currProd becomes 00. You're trying to divide by this, so you get an error.

    → Reply
»

This is some text.

// this is code

#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
void fun(vector<int> v, int n){
    int cnta = 0;
    int cntl = 0;
    vector<int> fr;
    vector<int> bk;
    for(int i=0;i<n;i++){
        if(v[i] == 1){
            fr.push_back(cnta);
        }
        else{
            cnta++;
            fr.push_back(cnta);
        }
        if(v[n-i-1] == 1){
            bk.push_back(cntl);
        }
        else{
            cntl++;
            bk.push_back(cntl);
        }
    }
    reverse(bk.begin(),bk.end());
    int ans = -1;
    for(int i=0;i<n-1;i++){
        if(fr[i] == bk[i+1]){
            ans = i + 1;
            break;
        }
    }
    cout<<ans<<endl;
}

The rest of the text.

solution in O(N) :) for question A

→ Reply

8 months ago, hide # |
 
0

For A I used Prefix product of the array in reverse order like start from right side and multiply it till it reaches left side of the array while storing that current product in the array! And now we can traverse array while multiplying the current element and checking if the next index element in prefix product is same or not If same then that is our k else we will continue till end Its O(N) TC with O(N) SC
